snippet.title: Emma O'Rourke: Painting Memories in Light and Shade (and Knicker Pink)
snippet.description:
Sub-titled video of The Plastic Podcasts interview with Emma O'Rourke

London-born artist Emma O’Rourke uses her own personal archive – and those of other people and institutions – to negotiate that most Irish of concerns: memory and remembrance. Whether sourcing family photos, the recollections of strangers or museum artefacts, her abstractions are both warm and bold. We talk about the effects of interpreting memory along with family, class, holidays and the “best colour”. 

Plus Declan McSweeney raises James “Big Jim” Larkin onto The Plastic Pedestal.

www.plasticpodcasts.com
@theplasticpodcasts1655

snippet.title: Clive James   Postcard From London
snippet.description:
..
First transmitted in 1991, Clive James recalls his experiences in 1960s London and trades anecdotes with Victoria Wood, Terence Donovan, Peter Cook and Michael Caine along the way.
Clive James  (7 October 1939 – 24 November 2019) was an Australian critic, journalist, broadcaster, writer and lyricist who lived and worked in the United Kingdom from 1962 until his death in 2019.He began his career specialising in literary criticism before becoming television critic for The Observer in 1972, where he made his name for his wry, deadpan humour.

snippet.title: Marc Ó Sé on drowning his sorrows, what Kerry are missing and some Páidí Ó Sé classics
snippet.description:
Kerry legend Marc Ó Sé joined Lee Costello and Niall McIntyre in studio to discuss his county's loss to Dublin in the All-Ireland senior football final.

The GAA JOE All-Star Football Team of the Year 2023 has officially been named, and as we all know, this accolade is the highest honour you can get.

Unlike the RTE All-Stars, we try to pick the team based on performances throughout the year, and some massive calls had to be made.

Many more deserved to be on the team, and some can count themselves unlucky not to be, but those who did make this starting line-up have made it on merit.

Dublin are the All-Ireland champions once again, and take lions share of this team, but there is still room for representatives from Kerry, Derry and Monaghan.

- Stephen Cluxton
- Conor McCloskey
- Mick Fitzsimons
- Tom O'Sullivan
- James McCarthy
- Karl O'Connell
- Conor McCarthy
- Brian Fenton
- Brendan Rogers
- Paudie Clifford
- Sean O'Shea
- Cormac Costello
- Shane McGuigan
- David Clifford
- Colm Basquel

snippet.title: a chat w/ JAMES PLOTKIN (Khanate, O.L.D, Scorn, Phantomsmasher and many more!) AA Podcast E 167
snippet.description:
Episode 167 
is a chat w/ JAMES PLOTKIN
James is one of the most prolific underground artists of the last 30+ years, from his beginnings in the grindcore scene with Regurgitation in 1987, through OLD, Scorn, a myriad of solo and collaborative releases, from digital hardcore to dark ambient and many points between, Phantomsmasher, Khlyst, Jodis and now to a new Khanate album. He also is well known for producing, mixing, remixing and mastering for bands such as Sunn O))), Pelican, Isis, Earth and Nadja.....among many many more!
This is a very cool look into the life and times of a genuine outsider and groundbreaker within the underground music scene and industry!!

snippet.title: Hurling needs a middle class and football S&C puzzle | Tyrrell and Tubridy | RTÉ GAA podcast
snippet.description:
It is the opening weekend of the Allianz Hurling League and the number of new managers does add intrigue to a competition that has seen its reputation take a bit of a buffeting in recent years.
Jackie is intrigued to see how Derek Lyng gets on in his first year as Brian Cody's successor in Kilkenny, with his old team-mate expecting to see an emphasis placed on youth.
Cork open against Limerick, which in recent seasons would suggest two points for the Rebels, as John Kiely's priorities have not been on the warm-up competition in recent years.
The league's issue, reckons Michael, is hurling's lack of a middle class to push the top teams. Unlike in football, there are few ambitious counties to challenge the established order, so the competition lacks jeopardy.
That said the likes of Henry Shefflin in Galway and Darragh Egan in Wexford will make good use of the league, even if they have no real ambition to win it.
FOOTBALL FROM 32 MINUTES
Retired Clare forward and all-time leading scorer in the Allianz Football League David Tubridy joins us to discuss round 2 of the league, where the intrigue and jeopardy in Division 2 just goes up a notch.
In the top tier, Tyrone v Donegal and Armagh v Mayo stand out as a pair of potentially significant and exciting matches.
David gives us some insight into the strength and conditioning work being done at this time of the year, while we also discuss the latest in the Glen-Kilmacud saga.
Web http://www.rte.ie/sport
Twitter: http://www.Twitter.com/rtesport

snippet.title: Harry & Meghan (2022) - Netflix - Docuseries - My Thoughts #harryandmeghan
snippet.description:
Harry & Meghan. Watch the Netflix Global Event. Starting December 8, only on Netflix.

Volume I: December 8
Volume II: December 15

In an unprecedented and in-depth documentary series, The Duke and Duchess of Sussex share the other side of their high-profile love story. Across six episodes, the series explores the clandestine days of their early courtship and the challenges that led to them feeling forced to step back from their full-time roles in the institution. With commentary from friends and family, most of whom have never spoken publicly before about what they witnessed, and historians who discuss the state of the British Commonwealth today and the royal family’s relationship with the press, the series does more than illuminate one couple’s love story, it paints a picture of our world and how we treat each other. From the critically-acclaimed, two-time Academy Award-nominated and Emmy-winning director Liz Garbus, Harry & Meghan is a never-before-seen look at one of the most-discussed couples in history.

snippet.title: Oscar, Jack O’Rourke
snippet.description:
Listen and stream here - https://open.spotify.com/album/1ibMxgsMNdy3zLYVZgl66z?si=LpJmz13jQOiepD93qYGIfw
Words, Brendan Behan, Ulik O Connor) 
Music, Jack O’Rourke 

“Oscar” Release Date: February 10th


Filmed By Bob Gallagher at Centre Culturel Irlandaise, Paris 

Vocals and piano: Jack O’Rourke

Fiddle: Conor Caldwell


“ The young king of Beauty, Narcissus broken.” 


After releasing and touring his critically acclaimed album Wild Place, Jack O’Rourke opens 2023 with the folk song “Oscar.” Jack composed music to this beautiful Brendan Behan poem, which captures Oscar Wilde’s final days in Paris with poignancy and wit.

 “Oscar”  is released on the week of the 7th February, the Centenary of Behan’s birth, 

Folk and traditional music were inspirational in the music composition – a haunting vocal melody with piano and cello, recorded at The Triskel, Cork. Fiddle is provided by Conor Caldwell and vocal harmonies by filmmaker and Sean nós singer, Bob Gallagher. Jack’s cellist, Aisling Fitzpatrick’s son is named Oscar, adding another dimension! 

 “Oscar” is the first release from a song cycle Jack composed during his Artistic Residency at Centre Culturel Irlandaise in Paris during July 2022. Researching Behan and Wilde”s existence in the city of Light, Jack found that these complex geniuses shared many links – sexuality, secrets and a safe haven in a more liberated environment than Ireland at the time.

Ultimately, both found solace, freedom and a few pints with Parisian flair. A couple of early houses Behan frequented were also explored by Jack for musical inspiration! 

Jack writes, “Ulick O’Connor’s translation from Behan’s Irish poem “Oscar “ captures the proud, tragic passing of Oscar Wilde. I’m teaching the Picture of Dorian Gray to my sixth years and Wilde’s genius and humour and layers of meaning are universal. I might try them on Behan’s Borstel Boy next!” 

“Bob Gallagher singing harmony and Conor Caldwell playing fiddle is a very personal addition – both were on residency with me at CCI in Paris and we’d never met before. Our daily collaboration and blending of notes in July were instrumental. It’s fitting that they appear on “Oscar.”

“I also wished to show another side of Behan, the poet. I love his humour but beyond the wit, and roguishness, is this beautiful vulnerability, He must have felt a kinship with Wilde when he finished the poem with, “my life on you Oscar boy, yourself had it both ways.” 

“In a way, I can relate… I think all of us can to life’s vicissitudes, the glories and falls from grace Behan describes.”

Jack is working on a show with Stephen James Smith and these featured musicians, to bring this song cycle to the stage. 

Jack hopes both Behan and Wilde would approve. 



Oscar 

Words: Brendan Behan

Translation: Ulik O’Connor  

Music: Jack O’Rourke
